_sources.citation: CBO, 'Estimated Impact of the American Recovery and Reinvestment Act on Employment and Economic Output' (Feb 2015)
_sources.notes: Table 2 multiplier ranges assume ZLB / accommodative Fed (2009–2015). Signs reverse for spending cuts. These are upper-end estimates for today's non-ZLB environment.

_sources.citation: Zidar, O. (2019). Tax Cuts For Whom? Heterogeneous Effects of Income Tax Changes on Growth and Employment. Journal of Political Economy, 127(3), 1437–1472.
_sources.notes: Exploits state-level variation in who benefits from federal tax changes. Finds near-zero employment/output effect for top-10% tax cuts; large effect for bottom 90%.

_sources.citation: Joint Committee on Taxation, 'Macroeconomic Analysis of the Tax Cuts and Jobs Act' (Dec 2017), JCX-69-17
_sources.notes: Full macro model scoring of the 2017 TCJA. Overall dynamic feedback offset ~26% of static cost, concentrated in top brackets. Per-bracket behavioral offset estimates derived from distributional analysis.

_sources.citation: Saez, E., Slemrod, J., & Giertz, S. (2012). The Elasticity of Taxable Income with Respect to Marginal Tax Rates: A Critical Review. Journal of Economic Literature, 50(1), 3–50.
_sources.notes: Authoritative survey of taxable income elasticity literature. ETI 0.12–0.40 for most earners; 0.5–1.5+ for top earners with high capital income. Per-bracket behavioral offset estimates are consistent with implied avoidance at these ETI values.

_sources.citation: CBO, 'The Long-Run Effects of Federal Budget Deficits on National Saving, Private Domestic Investment, and International Capital Flows' (2013), Working Paper 2014-02
_sources.notes: Neoclassical growth model calibration. Central estimate: $1 deficit increase crowds out $0.33 of domestic investment. Symmetric: deficit reduction raises investment $0.15–$0.50 (central $0.33). GDP effect ~$0.50 via capital accumulation by year 10.

_sources.citation: Laubach, T. (2009). New Evidence on the Interest Rate Effects of Budget Deficits and Debt. Journal of the European Economic Association, 7(4), 858–885.
_sources.notes: Uses 5-year-ahead CBO projections to avoid endogeneity. Finds ~25bp increase in real long-term rates per 1% of GDP increase in projected deficits. Debt level effect: 3–4bp per 1% of GDP debt.

_sources.citation: Barro, R. & Redlick, C. (2011). Macroeconomic Effects From Government Purchases and Taxes. Quarterly Journal of Economics, 126(1), 51–102.
_sources.notes: Narrative approach using US defense spending 1939–2008. Contemporaneous spending multiplier 0.4–0.5; over 2 years 0.6–0.7. Tax multiplier ~−1.1. Non-ZLB, normal-times benchmark.

_sources.citation: Ramey, V. & Zubairy, S. (2018). Government Spending Multipliers in Good Times and in Bad: Evidence from US Historical Data. Journal of Political Economy, 126(2), 850–901.
_sources.notes: 100+ years of quarterly US data. Spending multipliers below 1 in all regime specifications. Finds no evidence of elevated multipliers near ZLB, directly contradicting CER (2011). Key counterweight to ZLB-era estimates.

_sources.citation: Auerbach, A. & Gorodnichenko, Y. (2012). Measuring the Output Responses to Fiscal Policy. American Economic Journal: Economic Policy, 4(2), 1–27.
_sources.notes: Regime-switching VAR for US 1947–2008. Multipliers substantially larger in recessions than expansions. Key state-dependent benchmark. Expansion multipliers near 0; recession multipliers near 1–1.5.

_sources.citation: Gechert, S. (2015). What Fiscal Policy is Most Effective? A Meta-Regression Analysis. Oxford Economic Papers, 67(3), 553–580.
_sources.notes: Pools 89 published studies. Mean multipliers: transfers 0.39, tax cuts 0.52, general government spending 0.97, public investment 1.27. Controls for study design and publication bias.

_sources.citation: Ilzetzki, E., Mendoza, E., & Végh, C. (2013). How Big (Small?) Are Fiscal Multipliers? Journal of Monetary Economics, 60(2), 239–254.
_sources.notes: 44-country panel. High-debt countries (>60% GDP): long-run multiplier −2.3. Fixed exchange rate regimes: multiplier > 1. Flexible FX (US): near zero. Key reference for debt-level nonlinearity.

_sources.citation: IMF Fiscal Affairs Department (2014). Fiscal Multipliers: Size, Determinants, and Use in Macroeconomic Projections. Technical Notes and Manuals 14/04.
_sources.notes: Policy-facing summary for advanced economies. Average first-year spending multiplier 0.75; revenue multiplier 0.25. Hierarchy: government investment > purchases > wages > untargeted transfers.

_sources.citation: Romer, C. & Romer, D. (2010). The Macroeconomic Effects of Tax Changes: Estimates Based on a New Measure of Fiscal Shocks. American Economic Review, 100(3), 763–801.
_sources.notes: Narrative identification of exogenous US tax changes. Aggregate tax multiplier ~−3.0 at 3-year peak. Does not break out by income bracket; dominated by middle-income dynamics. Not directly applicable to top-bracket-only increases.

_sources.citation: Blanchard, O. & Leigh, D. (2013). Growth Forecast Errors and Fiscal Multipliers. IMF Working Paper WP/13/1; American Economic Review, 103(3), 117–120.
_sources.notes: European advanced economies 2010–2011. IMF assumed multiplier of 0.5; implied actual multiplier ~1.5 based on forecast errors. Evidence for elevated multipliers during fiscal consolidation in a ZLB/crisis environment.

spending_multipliers.source: CBO ARRA analysis (Feb 2015), Table 2 — transfer payments to individuals range 0.4–2.1; lower end used. See also: Gechert (2015) meta-analysis mean for transfers: 0.39.

alt_estimates.source: Gechert (2015) meta-analysis, mean for transfer payments
Barro & Redlick (2011) lower bound, non-ZLB normal times

spending_multipliers.notes: Recipients are predominantly retirees with higher saving rates than working-age low-income households. Lower end of CBO transfer range. ZLB caveat applies — non-ZLB estimate closer to 0.4–0.6.

spending_multipliers.source: CBO ARRA analysis (Feb 2015), Table 2 — transfer payments to individuals; lowest-end value used. Payments flow to providers, not directly to high-MPC households.

alt_estimates.source: Gechert (2015) meta-analysis, mean for transfers

spending_multipliers.notes: Payments flow to healthcare providers, not directly to high-MPC households. Lowest end of CBO transfer range. ZLB caveat applies.

spending_multipliers.source: CBO ARRA analysis (Feb 2015), Table 2 — transfer payments to states (other), range 0.4–1.8; near midpoint used. Medicaid is primarily a state fiscal relief mechanism.

alt_estimates.source: Gechert (2015) meta-analysis, mean for transfers

spending_multipliers.notes: Medicaid flows to states first; state fiscal relief range applies. ZLB caveat applies.

spending_multipliers.source: CBO ARRA analysis (Feb 2015), Table 2 — transfer payments to individuals; upper end used. CBO specifically noted UI extensions as highest-multiplier ARRA provision. Consistent with Auerbach & Gorodnichenko (2012) recession multiplier range of 1.0–1.5.

alt_estimates.source: Auerbach & Gorodnichenko (2012), AEJ:EP — recession lower bound
Gechert (2015) meta-analysis mean for transfers (pooled; likely underweights high-MPC programs)

spending_multipliers.notes: UI and SNAP recipients have very high marginal propensity to consume. CBO ARRA reports specifically flagged UI as among highest-multiplier provisions. Upper end estimate; non-ZLB estimate closer to 1.0.

spending_multipliers.cbo_category: Government purchases of goods and services
spending_multipliers.source: CBO ARRA analysis (Feb 2015), Table 2 — government purchases 0.5–2.5; conservative mid-range value used. Defense-specific literature: Barro & Redlick (2011, QJE) 0.4–0.6 contemporaneous, 0.6–0.7 over 2 years; Ramey (2011, AER) 0.6–1.2.

alt_estimates.source: Barro & Redlick (2011, QJE) — narrative defense spending, non-ZLB
Ramey (2011, AER) — upper range, defense narrative identification
Gechert (2015) meta-analysis, mean for general government spending

spending_multipliers.notes: Defense-specific literature finds 0.6–1.2 range. Uses 1.0 as midpoint consistent with both Ramey (2011) and CBO range. Non-ZLB estimate likely 0.5–0.8.

spending_multipliers.source: CBO ARRA analysis (Feb 2015), Table 2 — transfer payments to individuals; mid-range used. Mix of direct compensation payments and healthcare services.

alt_estimates.source: Gechert (2015) meta-analysis mean for transfers

spending_multipliers.notes: Mix of direct cash payments and healthcare services. Mid-range of CBO transfer category.

spending_multipliers.category: Education, Training, Employment, and Social Services
spending_multipliers.source: CBO ARRA analysis (Feb 2015), Table 2 — transfer payments to states (other), range 0.4–1.8; midpoint used. Note: IMF (2015, Abiad et al.) finds public investment specifically crowds IN private investment with larger long-run multiplier of ~2.5.

alt_estimates.source: Gechert (2015) meta-analysis, mean for public investment spending
IMF Abiad, Furceri & Topalova (2015) — public investment, long-run effect

spending_multipliers.notes: Federal education and training grants flow primarily to states. Some portion is genuine public investment with potentially higher long-run multiplier. Midpoint used; Gechert investment estimate may be more appropriate for education capital spending.

spending_multipliers.cbo_category: Government purchases of goods and services
spending_multipliers.source: CBO ARRA analysis (Feb 2015), Table 2 — government purchases, range 0.5–2.5; mid-range used. Residual discretionary spending; composition unknown.

alt_estimates.source: IMF 2014 Technical Note (TNM 14/04) — average advanced economy spending multiplier
Gechert (2015) meta-analysis, mean for general government spending

spending_multipliers.notes: Residual discretionary category; true composition unknown. Mid-range estimate. IMF 0.75 average is a reasonable non-ZLB alternative.

tax_multipliers.source: CBO ARRA analysis (Feb 2015), Table 2 — lower/middle income tax provisions, range 0.4–1.8; upper end used. Consistent with Zidar (2019, JPE) finding of large employment effects for bottom-90% income tax changes.

alt_estimates.source: Romer & Romer (2010, AER) implied aggregate — weighted toward middle income
Gechert (2015) meta-analysis mean for tax cuts (pooled across brackets)

tax_multipliers.notes: High-MPC households with predominantly wage income and minimal avoidance capacity. Upper end of CBO range; ZLB caveat applies. Non-ZLB estimate closer to 1.0–1.2.

tax_multipliers.source: CBO ARRA analysis (Feb 2015), Table 2 — lower/middle income tax provisions; midpoint used. Core middle-income bracket; consistent with Romer & Romer (2010, AER) aggregate (dominated by middle-income dynamics).

alt_estimates.source: Romer & Romer (2010, AER) aggregate 3-year peak — likely upper bound for this bracket
Gechert (2015) meta-analysis mean for tax cuts

tax_multipliers.notes: Midpoint of CBO range. Romer-Romer aggregate of −3.0 is peak at 3 years, not year-1; year-1 implied is closer to 1.0–1.5. ZLB caveat applies.

tax_multipliers.source: CBO ARRA analysis (Feb 2015), Table 2 — lower/middle income provisions; lower portion used reflecting higher saving rate. Approaching Zidar (2019) top-10% boundary where effects attenuate.

alt_estimates.source: Gechert (2015) meta-analysis mean for tax cuts (pooled)
CBO ARRA lower bound for this category

tax_multipliers.notes: Upper-middle income; higher saving rate, growing access to tax sheltering. Lower portion of CBO range.

tax_multipliers.source: CBO ARRA analysis (Feb 2015), Table 2 — high-income/corporate provisions, range 0.0–0.4; upper bound used as these are individuals (not corporations) with some consumption response. Consistent with Zidar (2019, JPE): near-zero employment effect for top-10% income changes.

alt_estimates.source: Zidar (2019, JPE) — near-zero GDP effect for top-10% income group
Gechert (2015) meta mean for tax cuts (caution: pooled across brackets, dominated by middle income)

tax_multipliers.notes: High income; growing income-shifting capacity. Upper bound of CBO's corporate/high-income range used for individual earners.

tax_multipliers.source: CBO ARRA analysis (Feb 2015), Table 2 — high-income/corporate provisions; lower end used reflecting maximum avoidance capacity. Consistent with Zidar (2019, JPE) near-zero top-bracket result.

alt_estimates.source: Zidar (2019, JPE) — near-zero for top-10% income group; this bracket is solidly top-0.1%

tax_multipliers.notes: Top income group. Strongest income-shifting and deferral behavior (carried interest, capital gains timing, offshore vehicles). Low end of CBO range.

behavioral_offsets.source: JCT JCX-69-17 (2017) implied distribution; Saez, Slemrod & Giertz (2012, JEL) ETI survey — ETI near 0.05–0.10 for low-income wage earners implies ~5% offset at moderate rate increases.
behavioral_offsets.notes: Minimal avoidance channels: predominantly W-2 wage income, few retirement or business income options. ETI literature (Saez et al. 2012) consistently finds near-zero ETI at bottom of income distribution.

behavioral_offsets.source: JCT JCX-69-17 (2017) implied distribution; Saez, Slemrod & Giertz (2012, JEL) — ETI ~0.10–0.20 for middle-income earners implies 8–12% offset.
behavioral_offsets.notes: Some ability to adjust 401(k) contributions, itemized deductions, and timing of bonus income. ETI survey (Saez et al. 2012) midpoint for this bracket.

behavioral_offsets.source: JCT JCX-69-17 (2017) implied distribution; Saez, Slemrod & Giertz (2012, JEL) — ETI ~0.20–0.35 for upper-middle earners. Pass-through income and self-employment begin to matter.
behavioral_offsets.notes: Moderate avoidance capacity: retirement accounts, itemized deductions, some pass-through income timing.

behavioral_offsets.source: JCT JCX-69-17 (2017) — overall 26% dynamic offset concentrated in top brackets; Saez, Slemrod & Giertz (2012, JEL) — ETI 0.40–0.60 for high-income earners with substantial capital and business income.
behavioral_offsets.notes: Significant income-shifting capacity: pass-through entity elections, deferred compensation, capital gains realization timing, charitable giving strategies.

behavioral_offsets.source: JCT JCX-69-17 (2017) — ~26% overall dynamic offset with heaviest concentration at top; Saez, Slemrod & Giertz (2012, JEL) — ETI 0.5–1.5 for top earners with high capital income; Kopczuk (2005) finds high ETI for very-top earners driven by avoidance.
behavioral_offsets.notes: Maximum avoidance capacity: carried interest treatment, offshore deferral, charitable vehicles, capital gains timing. JCT TCJA aggregate offset (~26%) is predominantly concentrated here and at $200K–$1M bracket.

crowding_in.source: CBO Working Paper 2014-02 (2013) — neoclassical growth model calibration. Central: $1 deficit reduction → $0.33 domestic investment → ~$0.50 GDP by year 10. Interest rate channel: Laubach (2009, JEEA) ~25bp rate reduction per 1% of GDP deficit reduction. Open-economy adjustment: US floats FX and draws global capital, reducing domestic investment response; Ilzetzki, Mendoza & Végh (2013, JME) find near-zero multipliers in flexible-FX advanced economies.

alt_estimates.source: Lower bound: open-economy + r<g environment (Blanchard 2019, AEA) — government borrowing competes in global capital market; real rate already below growth rate
Open-economy adjustment to CBO central: Ilzetzki et al. (2013) suggests flexible-FX economies have substantially smaller domestic crowding effects
CBO 2013 central estimate — closed-economy Solow model, full-employment benchmark
Upper bound: high-debt environment with meaningful risk premium channel; Laubach (2009) interest rate sensitivity implies larger investment response at current debt levels

crowding_in.notes: CBO central estimate ($0.50) assumes a closed economy at full employment — likely an upper bound for the US. Open-economy adjustment (~0.30) more appropriate given floating dollar and global capital integration. Composition caveat: cuts to public investment (infrastructure, R&D, education) may crowd OUT private capital rather than in — the $0.50 figure applies to transfer cuts, not investment cuts.
